paper.title: Finite Scalar Quantization: VQ-VAE Made Simple

problem_statement: Vector quantization in VQ-VAEs suffers from codebook collapse (many codes go unused), requires complex training tricks (EMA updates, codebook reset, commitment losses), and the straight-through estimator introduces gradient bias. We want discrete representations for generative modeling but the quantization step is fragile and hard to train. Is there a simpler way to get discrete latent codes that avoids these problems entirely?
known_ingredients: VQ-VAE uses a learned codebook of embedding vectors and maps encoder outputs to the nearest codebook entry
The straight-through estimator copies gradients through the non-differentiable argmin operation, introducing bias
Codebook collapse means many codebook entries are never selected, wasting capacity
Scalar quantization (rounding real numbers to integers) is a well-known signal processing technique
Product quantization decomposes a vector into independent sub-vectors and quantizes each separately
The Gumbel-Softmax trick provides another way to handle discrete variables but adds temperature scheduling complexity
key_insight: Instead of learning a codebook and doing vector quantization, simply round each scalar dimension of the encoder output to the nearest integer within a fixed range (e.g., round to {0,1,2,3,4} per dimension). This 'finite scalar quantization' gives discrete codes with zero codebook collapse (all levels are structurally available), needs no commitment loss or EMA, and the straight-through estimator on simple rounding has much less gradient bias than on argmin-to-codebook. It matches or beats VQ-VAE quality with dramatically simpler training.
insight_components: Replace vector quantization with per-dimension scalar rounding to fixed integer levels
This eliminates codebook collapse by construction since there is no learned codebook
The straight-through estimator on scalar rounding is simpler and lower-bias than on vector argmin
The effective codebook size is the product of levels across dimensions, giving exponentially large implicit codebooks
difficulty_rationale: The ingredients mention scalar quantization and product quantization separately, but connecting them to replace VQ entirely — and realizing this solves codebook collapse by construction — requires a non-obvious reframing of the problem.
